video_title: Tech Tumble Overshadows Markets' Iran Hopes | The Asia Trade 6/23/2026
gist: Bloomberg's Asia Trade covers a tech selloff led by Alphabet (worst day in 13 months on a key researcher's exit to Anthropic) and SpaceX's first-ever $20B investment-grade bond sale to fund AI, which is pressuring Treasury yields. A hawkish Fed under Kevin Warsh is repricing rate-hike expectations forward to September, lifting the dollar to multi-decade highs vs the yen, while a fragile US-Iran deal lets Iran sell oil again and Korean leveraged ETF mania draws regulator concern.

themes.theme: AI Capex Financing Boom and Hyperscaler Debt Supply

insights.claim: SpaceX is launching its first-ever investment-grade bond sale seeking at least $20 billion with maturities of 5 to 30 years to fund AI ambitions, an unusual move for a company that is not yet profitable and will burn cash through 2029.
insights.speaker: Norah Mulinda (Bloomberg equities reporter)
insights.so_what: A loss-making issuer tapping the high-grade market signals AI capex is so large that even premium-valued firms prefer debt over diluting equity, adding heavy supply to credit and rates.
insights.quote: WE KNOW THE COMPANY IS GOING TO BE BURNING CASH THROUGH 2029.

insights.claim: SpaceX has shed roughly $600 billion in market cap over three days (more than 20%) following its bond-sale announcement, having raised debt rather than equity precisely because its equity is overvalued by any metric.
insights.speaker: Anthony Stevens / Norah Mulinda (Bloomberg)
insights.so_what: Investors are repricing AI infrastructure plays as the cost and scale of the buildout becomes clear, a warning for over-extended AI equity valuations.
insights.quote: THE FACT THAT SPACEX IS RAISING DEBT INSTEAD OF EQUITY WHEN THE EQUITY IS SO OVERVALUED BY ANY METRIC INDICATES THAT SPACEX IS EMBARKING ON ITS OWN PART OF THE ARMS RACE

insights.claim: Hyperscalers have raised about $300 billion for AI since November of last year across multiple credit markets, with Alphabet, Amazon and others all issuing.
insights.speaker: Sherry (anchor, citing Bloomberg data)
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale of AI-driven credit issuance that is now a structural driver of Treasury yields and credit spreads.
insights.quote: $300 BILLION FROM HIGHS TO A.I. SINCE NOVEMBER OF LAST YEAR, ACROSS MULTIPLE CREDIT MARKETS

insights.claim: The biggest market theme is simultaneous equity and debt issuance from the seven mega-cap hyperscalers and their related mega-IPOs, which is likely to drive a mild market correction as the market digests the extra supply while staying around current levels.
insights.speaker: Daniel Lam (head of cross-asset derivative strategy)
insights.so_what: Frames near-term equity downside as a supply/digestion event rather than a fundamental break, suggesting buying corrections rather than exiting.
insights.quote: THE MARKET IS PROBABLY GOING TO HAVE A LITTLE BIT OF CORRECTION, NOT A LOT, ALL STAYING AROUND THE CURRENT LEVELS.

insights.claim: SpaceX signed a multibillion-dollar deal for Reflection AI to pay $50 million per month through 2029 for SpaceX data-center compute, part of SpaceX's push to sell computing capacity to AI firms.
insights.speaker: Sherry (anchor)
insights.so_what: Shows SpaceX building recurring compute-as-a-service revenue to justify its AI capex and bond raise, a new competitive front in cloud/AI infrastructure.

themes.theme: Hawkish Fed, Rate Repricing and Treasury Yields

insights.claim: Investors have aggressively repriced the expected first Fed rate hike from March next year (priced a week ago) forward to about September, bracing for a more hawkish Fed under Chairman Kevin Warsh after last week's FOMC meeting.
insights.speaker: Ruth Carson (chief Asia FX and rates correspondent)
insights.so_what: A faster-than-expected hawkish hike timeline is the dominant cross-asset driver, lifting front-end yields, the dollar, and pressuring high-growth equities.
insights.quote: THIS TIME LAST WEEK INVESTORS SURPRISING IN A FED RATE HIKE IN MARCH NEXT YEAR. NOW THEY HAVE PULLED IT TO ABOUT SEPTEMBER.

insights.claim: The 2s10s curve has flattened to about a 27 basis point spread driven by the front end, where two-year yields are highly sensitive to the Fed path, with room for yields to run higher if the US economy is judged to be hot.
insights.speaker: Ruth Carson (Bloomberg)
insights.so_what: Identifies the front-end-led flattening as the read on Fed expectations and flags further upside risk to yields on strong data.
insights.quote: THE FLATTENING OF THE CURVE HAS ACTUALLY COME FROM THE FRONT END.

insights.claim: The upcoming $20B SpaceX bond offering will itself weigh on Treasury yields because investors must pull money from somewhere, including the rates side, to fund the new supply.
insights.speaker: Ruth Carson (Bloomberg)
insights.so_what: Links corporate AI issuance directly to higher Treasury yields, a crowding-out dynamic investors should monitor as more hyperscaler debt comes.
insights.quote: WILL THIS WEIGH ON TREASURY YIELDS? IT WILL.

insights.claim: Warsh's speech focused on controlling inflation over growth and provided less forward guidance, prompting the market to err on the side of caution rather than going gung-ho, which weighs on high-growth areas.
insights.speaker: Daniel Lam (cross-asset derivative strategist)
insights.so_what: Reduced forward guidance plus inflation focus raises the risk premium on long-duration/high-growth equities.

themes.theme: Semiconductors vs Hyperscalers and the Memory Trade

insights.claim: Despite the Nasdaq falling 1.3% and Alphabet down 5%, the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index rose for a third straight session, with gains concentrated in semis while hyperscalers and mega-caps sold off.
insights.speaker: Anthony Stevens (Bloomberg markets reporter)
insights.so_what: Chips are decoupling from hyperscalers and proving insulated from higher yields, the key intra-tech rotation to trade.
insights.quote: MOST OF THE GAINS WERE CONCENTRATED IN THE SEMICONDUCTOR SPACE.

insights.claim: As the memory trade gets materially repriced higher, second-tier and smaller players with commoditized memory in Taiwan and China will do well next, a positive dynamic for the Asian supply chain.
insights.speaker: Anthony Stevens (Bloomberg)
insights.so_what: Suggests rotation down the memory quality curve into smaller commoditized players as the cycle broadens.
insights.quote: AS EVERY SECTION OF THE MEMORY TRADE STARTS TO GET MATERIALLY REPRICED HIGHER YOU START TO SEE THE SMALLER PLAYERS WITH COMMODITIZED MEMORY DO WELL.

insights.claim: Micron traded almost twice the entire Korean market volume yesterday, and Micron's earnings (Wednesday) will reveal whether memory makers' profits are cyclical or structural and how far out earnings visibility extends.
insights.speaker: Bloomberg markets reporter (Korea)
insights.so_what: Micron earnings are the key catalyst that will validate or break the memory-supercycle thesis underpinning Korean chip valuations.
insights.quote: MICRON WAS ALMOST TWICE THE ENTIRE KOREAN MARKET VOLUME YESTERDAY.

insights.claim: Hyperscalers are now lining up with individual memory suppliers to lock down supply, as evidenced by the Anthropic memory deal, a structurally positive dynamic for the Asian memory supply chain.
insights.speaker: Anthony Stevens (Bloomberg)
insights.so_what: Direct supplier lock-ins point to sustained, contracted memory demand rather than a spot-price spike, supporting structural earnings visibility.

insights.claim: Korea is cheap on an absolute level considering memory makers' vast profits, with Korean futures up 1.8% even after the local regulator talked down leveraged ETFs.
insights.speaker: Bloomberg markets reporter (Korea)
insights.so_what: Frames Korea as fundamentally cheap relative to memory earnings, but valuation hinges on whether those profits are structural.
insights.quote: ON AN ABSOLUTE LEVEL THE MARKET IS CHEAP CONSIDERING THE VAST PROFITS THE MEMORY MAKERS ARE MAKING

themes.theme: Alphabet, the AI Talent War and Frontier Models

insights.claim: Google's market cap dropped about $250 billion after a key researcher (Nobel laureate John Jumper) departed for Anthropic, marking Alphabet's worst day in roughly 13 months.
insights.speaker: Bloomberg Intelligence global head of technology research
insights.so_what: Quantifies how a single frontier-AI researcher's exit can move $250B of value, underscoring concentrated key-person risk in mega-cap AI.
insights.quote: I DON'T KNOW IF RESEARCHERS ARE WORK $250 BILLION, BUT THAT'S HOW MUCH GOOGLES MARKET CAP DROPPED AFTER THEIR DEPARTURE.

insights.claim: Google appears to have missed the agents wave and its model cadence has slowed since Flash 3.5 a few months ago, while Anthropic's latest release ('Mythos') leads the frontier and attracts both enterprise and government interest.
insights.speaker: Bloomberg Intelligence head of tech research
insights.so_what: Alphabet's competitive position depends on re-accelerating model releases to match Anthropic; a comparable model launch is the near-term catalyst to watch.
insights.quote: IT SEEMS LIKE GOOGLE HAS MISSED OUT ON THIS WHOLE WAVE OF AGENTS

insights.claim: Despite the talent loss, Alphabet has significant positives in its TPU business and chip backlog with Anthropic, with the TPU business doing very well.
insights.speaker: Bloomberg Intelligence head of tech research
insights.so_what: Alphabet's custom-silicon (TPU) franchise provides a buffer and revenue stream independent of its frontier-model cadence problem.

themes.theme: Dollar Strength, Yen at 40-Year Lows and Asia FX

insights.claim: The dollar is strengthening on the hawkish Fed narrative even as Iran peace progress would normally ease safe-haven demand, pushing the yen toward a 40-year low (161.95 was touched in 1986) and the Korean won near 1,500.
insights.speaker: Sherry / Anthony Stevens (Bloomberg)
insights.so_what: Fed repricing, not geopolitics, is the dominant FX driver, keeping Asian EM currencies under pressure regardless of risk sentiment.
insights.quote: 161.95 IS THE LEVEL WE'RE WATCHING OUT FOR... WE TOUCHED THAT IN 1986, SO THAT WOULD BE A 40-YEAR LOW

insights.claim: The cost and effectiveness of Bank of Japan intervention is getting lower, so the BOJ is carefully choosing the point to use its next bullet, making it very difficult for the yen to reverse while the dollar keeps rising.
insights.speaker: Daniel Lam (cross-asset derivative strategist)
insights.so_what: Diminishing intervention efficacy means investors should not expect a near-term BOJ-driven yen reversal absent a Fed pivot.
insights.quote: THE COST TO INTERVENE AND THE EFFECTIVENESS OF THEIR INTERVENTION IS ACTUALLY GETTING LESS

insights.claim: FX hedging tied to the attractiveness of Japanese equities is itself driving further yen downside, per Bank of America Securities research.
insights.speaker: Sherry (anchor, citing BofA Securities)
insights.so_what: Equity-hedging flows are a self-reinforcing source of yen weakness, distinct from rate differentials.

insights.claim: Japan's market is more diversified and lower-volatility than Korea's, which depends heavily on just Samsung and SK Hynix, so investors can gradually add Japan exposure but should wait for a correction before buying Korean equities at current levels.
insights.speaker: Daniel Lam (cross-asset derivative strategist)
insights.so_what: Actionable relative-value call: prefer diversified Japan for core exposure, treat Korea as a higher-beta trade to buy on dips.
insights.quote: IN JAPAN, I THINK ONE CAN GRADUALLY ADD EXPOSURE TO THAT MARKET.

insights.claim: The US issued a 60-day license allowing Iran to sell oil internationally (including to two US refiners), and roughly 30 million barrels moved out of the Strait of Hormuz last week as a wartime backlog cleared, before normalizing toward ~2 million barrels a day.
insights.speaker: Laura Davison (Bloomberg politics editor)
insights.so_what: Iranian barrels returning to market is a bearish supply catalyst for oil, with the initial 30M-barrel surge being a one-off backlog clearance.
insights.quote: WE SAW ABOUT 30 MILLION BARRELS MOVE OUT OF THE STRAIT OF HORMUZ LAST WEEK.

insights.claim: Conflicting US-Iran readouts on nuclear-inspector access and frozen-asset use signal the 14-point memorandum of understanding is fragile, and some sanctions waivers could be tweaked or delayed if Treasury is seen to have moved too quickly.
insights.speaker: Laura Davison (Bloomberg politics editor)
insights.so_what: Deal fragility and possible sanctions reversal keep an oil-supply tail risk alive despite the headline truce.

insights.claim: US gasoline has dropped back below $4 a gallon (the key benchmark it rose above at the war's start), but economists say it will take a long time for prices to fall further, possibly too late for Republicans before the November midterms.
insights.speaker: Laura Davison (Bloomberg politics editor)
insights.so_what: Lagging gasoline relief ties energy prices to US political risk and the midterm outcome.

insights.claim: China's economy shows three divergences: strong external demand (exports growing ~10% toward ~$3.5 trillion) versus soft internal retail sales and investment; new industries (high-tech, chips, EVs) versus declining old industries (real estate); and government infrastructure investment versus weak consumption.
insights.speaker: Jianguang (chief economist and VP, JD.com)
insights.so_what: A clear framework for China exposure: lean into exporters and new-economy/high-tech, avoid real estate and consumption-dependent names.
insights.quote: THERE IS A HUGE DIVERGENCE IN THE CHINESE ECONOMY. I PUT IT INTO THREE DIFFERENT... DIVERGENCES.

insights.claim: China's Q1 strength in retail and investment was a one-off and demand has yet to recover, with softening already visible in April; more fiscal support is expected after the July assessment of first-half data.
insights.speaker: Jianguang (chief economist, JD.com)
insights.so_what: Signals additional Chinese fiscal stimulus is coming in H2, a potential catalyst for domestic-demand-exposed assets.
insights.quote: I SAY IT WILL BE A ONE-OFF. I CAN SEE THE DEMAND HAS STILL YET TO RECOVER.

insights.claim: China's PPI has turned positive for the first time in four years and CPI is positive simultaneously, but the modest CPI rise is mostly imported (oil-driven) rather than demand-led, and consumption subsidies are now seeing payback as demand was pulled forward.
insights.speaker: Jianguang (chief economist, JD.com)
insights.so_what: Positive PPI marks a potential inflection out of deflation, but the imported nature warns the reflation is not yet self-sustaining domestic demand.
insights.quote: PPI HAS BEEN POSITIVE FOR THE FIRST TIME IN FOUR YEARS.

insights.claim: Chinese mid- and small-cap stocks are still doing well while big-cap China tech has been underclassed, because China tech is a diversified play without as much earnings growth as US tech, so investors chasing high-growth names favor the US.
insights.speaker: Daniel Lam (cross-asset derivative strategist)
insights.so_what: Explains China's lag versus the US and points investors toward Chinese mid/small caps over mega-cap tech for growth.

themes.theme: Leveraged ETF Mania and Market Structure Risk

insights.claim: A single leveraged ETF in Korea has overtaken the assets under management of a broad-based market ETF in its own country, with leveraged ETFs at record AUM now starting to break the underlying market microstructure via options-based exposure.
insights.speaker: Bloomberg markets reporter (Korea)
insights.so_what: Record leveraged-ETF AUM distorting the underlying market is a systemic volatility and tracking-error risk, prompting regulator warnings.
insights.quote: A SINGLE ETF AS OUTWEIGHED ASSETS UNDER MANAGEMENT OF A BROAD-BASED MARKET ETF IN ITS OWN COUNTRY.

insights.claim: More than 90% of holders of Korea's leveraged ETFs are retail investors, who are paying for performance rather than price-performance and will increasingly face lagging returns versus the underlying.
insights.speaker: Bloomberg markets reporter (Korea) / Sherry
insights.so_what: Heavy retail concentration in leveraged products that lag the underlying creates an asymmetric retail-loss and forced-deleveraging risk in a selloff.
insights.quote: MORE THAN 90% OF HOLDERS OF THOSE LEVERAGED ETF SEEM TO BE RETAIL INVESTORS.

themes.theme: Contrarian Macro: Tech Trumps the Fed

insights.claim: The tech/AI boom will have a greater economic impact than Fed policy, so whether rates are 50bp higher barely matters; the economy is powered by AI technology and does not depend much on policy rates.
insights.speaker: Remaining Macro Associates CEO/Chairman
insights.so_what: A contrarian call that the market is over-obsessed with the Fed; if right, dips driven by hawkish-Fed fears are buying opportunities.
insights.quote: PEOPLE OBSESSED WITH THE FED, WHETHER IT'S 50 BASIS POINTS HIGHER... WHAT IS THE DIFFERENCE? THE KEY STORY IS TECH BOOM.

insights.claim: Roughly 80% of current US GDP growth is linked to AI in one way or another, and other markets will grow toward that level over the next three to five years.
insights.speaker: Daniel Lam (cross-asset derivative strategist)
insights.so_what: Quantifies AI's outsized contribution to growth, reinforcing why investors cannot avoid AI exposure despite valuation concerns.
insights.quote: NOWADAYS 80% OF THE U.S. G.D.P. GROWTH IS LINKED TO A.I. IN ONE WAY OR ANOTHER.

insights.claim: Within US tech the next leg of the AI cycle will be internet stocks (volume-growth exposed) rather than semiconductors, which are turning more defensive after their run, mirroring the smartphone cycle where semis led and mature internet names followed.
insights.speaker: Daniel Lam (cross-asset derivative strategist)
insights.so_what: Actionable rotation call: shift from semis toward volume-driven internet names as the AI cycle matures.
insights.quote: THE NEXT AREA OF THIS A.I. CYCLE IS GOING TO BE THE INTERNET STOCKS.

themes.theme: Bird Flu and Australian Inflation Tail Risk

insights.claim: H5 bird flu has reached mainland Australia for the first time (two cases in Western Australia), and if it reaches dense commercial poultry/livestock operations it could trigger culls, shortages of chicken, eggs and milk, and add to inflation in staples.
insights.speaker: Angus Whitley (Bloomberg global business reporter)
insights.so_what: A new food-inflation tail risk for Australia just as the RBA watches CPI, with precedent from the US where the virus hit 700 California dairy herds across 16+ states.

insights.claim: This bird flu strain is more adapted to infect mammals than normal avian flu, causing up to 96% mortality in seal pups in some populations, though only a handful of human cases and no human-to-human transmission have occurred.
insights.speaker: Jane Younger (senior lecturer, University of Tasmania)
insights.so_what: High mammalian adaptation raises the longer-tail pandemic/agricultural risk that markets are not yet pricing.
insights.quote: IN SOME POPULATIONS WE HAVE SEEN 96% MORTALITY OF SEAL PUPS.
